name: Charco del Aceite Circular Trail
name_original: Charco la Pringue - circular por el P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
description: Hike the difficult 10.7-mile Charco la Pringue circular trail in Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas National Park, taking about 5 hours and 18 min
region_intro: Hiking around Sorihuela Del Guadalimar offers access to the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, Spain's largest protected natural area. The terrain features mountainous landscapes, river valleys, and extensive pine forests. The region is characterized by a mix of rugged mountains, karstic formations, and traditional olive groves. Numerous rivers, including the Guadalimar, contribute to the area's hydrological importance. Best hiking trails around Sorihuela Del Guadalimar The most popular hiking route is Charco del Aceite Circular Trail, a 10.7 miles (17.3 km) trail that takes about 5 hours 18 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the natural pools and riverbeds of the area. Another top favourite among local hikers is Ovens and the shores of the Tranco de Beas reservoir - circular through the Cazorla, Segura and Las Villas Sierras N.P., a moderate 4.4 miles (7.0 km) path. This trail offers views of the reservoir and surrounding natural park. Local hikers also love the Alto del Montero - circular through the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, a 11.3 miles (18.3 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 10 minutes. Hiking around Sorihuela Del Guadalimar is defined by mountainous landscapes, river valleys, and extensive pine forests. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes. The routes in Sorihuela Del Guadalimar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 90 hikers have used komoot to explore Sorihuela Del Guadalimar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around Sorihuela del Guadalimar?
A: Hiking around Sorihuela del Guadalimar offers a diverse landscape. You'll find mountainous terrain within the Sierra de las Cuatro Villas and the larger Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. Expect river valleys, extensive pine forests, karstic formations, and even traditional olive groves and cereal fields, providing a varied experience from rugged mountains to serene agricultural areas.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there are options for easier hikes. The region offers 2 easy routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21846883" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornos Castle and Village – Views of the Tranco reservoir loop from Hornos&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 6.3 km path. These trails are generally less demanding, making them suitable for those new to hiking or families looking for a gentle outing.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the trails?
A: The area is rich in natural features. You can explore spectacular natural pools and recreational areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3168204" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Aceite&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5411386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco de la Pringue&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4508600" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tranco Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; is another prominent feature, and you can find viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5446556" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Risca del Guijarrón&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6044069" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Huelguecillas Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; offering panoramic views.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions to visit while hiking?
A: Yes, the region blends natural beauty with historical elements. The settlement of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3896739" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornos&lt;/a&gt;, with its historic castle dating from the 12th to 14th centuries, is a notable attraction. You can also find the Arab Tower (Torreón Árabe) in the area, offering glimpses into the region's past.

Q: Are there any long-distance hiking routes in the area?
A: Sorihuela del Guadalimar is situated within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, which is home to the extensive GR 247 "Bosques del Sur" trail. This major circular long-distance route spans 478 kilometers, divided into 21 main stages, and includes bypasses and alternative variants, allowing for multi-day adventures through diverse ecosystems.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Sorihuela del Guadalimar?
A: The Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park offers good hiking conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant temperatures for exploring the trails. Summers can be hot, especially at lower elevations, while winters can bring snow to higher mountain areas, offering different challenges and scenic beauty.

Q: Are there places to stay or rest along the trails, such as mountain refuges?
A: Yes, along the GR 247 and other routes within the Natural Park, you can find facilities like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3286409" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa Forestal La Parra Refuge&lt;/a&gt;. These serve as resting points, offering basic amenities. While some stages connect to villages with services, planning for remote sections and carrying necessary supplies is advisable.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails?
A: The Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park generally allows dogs on trails, but it's crucial to keep them under control, preferably on a leash, to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Always check specific trail regulations or park guidelines before heading out, especially regarding protected areas or sensitive habitats.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Sorihuela del Guadalimar?
A: The hiking trails around Sorihuela del Guadalimar are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 10 reviews. Hikers often praise the stunning natural beauty, the variety of landscapes from riverbeds to mountainous terrain, and the immersive experience within Spain's largest protected natural area.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988169034/charco-del-aceite-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Aceite Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 17.3 km loop exploring natural pools, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988193569/alto-del-montero-circular-through-the-p-n-de-las-sierras-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Montero - circular through the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas&lt;/a&gt; is an 18.3 km difficult route through mountainous terrain.

Q: Is public transport available to reach trailheads, or is a car necessary?
A: While some villages within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park may have limited bus services, reaching many trailheads around Sorihuela del Guadalimar often requires a private vehicle. The region's vastness and mountainous terrain make public transport less comprehensive for accessing remote starting points. Parking is generally available near popular trailheads and villages.
tips:
The Charco del Aceite recreational area is located in a magnificent place to take a bath since it has a pool of water embedded in the same bed of the Guadalquivir river and pine forests on its slopes.
It has stone tables, wooden seats and a kiosk-bar.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to hike the Charco la Pringue circular trail?
faqs.answer: The full circular route is approximately 17.25 km long and generally takes around 5 hours and 20 minutes to complete. This duration can vary based on your pace and how much time you spend enjoying the natural pools and scenery.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this circular trail?
faqs.answer: This specific circular route is classified as difficult hiking, requiring very good physical condition and sure-footedness. While shorter, direct routes to Charco la Pringue are often considered low difficulty, the full circular trail through the National Park presents more challenging conditions.

faqs.question: Where is the trailhead and is parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail is located within the Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Sierras National Park. Parking is usually available near the recreational zone at Charco la Pringue. However, during peak times, it can fill up quickly, so arriving early is advisable.

faqs.question: Is the Charco la Pringue trail suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: The full circular trail is rated as difficult and may not be suitable for beginners or young children. However, shorter, more direct routes to the natural pool area are often considered low difficulty and accessible for all audiences, offering a great spot for relaxation and swimming.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the circular trail?
faqs.answer: The trail offers a diverse mix of terrain. You will encounter sections on paved tracks, natural paths, and unpaved tracks. It generally follows the Guadalquivir River, featuring lush riparian vegetation and some rocky areas.

faqs.question: What are the main natural features and viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: The highlight is the stunning natural pool, Charco del Aceite (also known as Charco la Pringue), with its crystal-clear waters. You'll also find a cave with ivy-covered walls nearby, and impressive views of peaks like Guijarrón, Poyo de Andaragasca, and Lancha del Tosero. Further downstream, you can observe the turbulent outflow of water from the El Tranco reservoir's drainage tunnel.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter along the trail?
faqs.answer: The area is rich in biodiversity. In the water, you might spot trout, barbel, bogas, and otters. On land, the park is home to deer, Iberian ibex, and wild boars, especially in the quieter sections of the trail.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The Charco la Pringue is particularly appealing during warmer months when its natural pool offers a refreshing escape for swimming. Spring and autumn also provide pleasant hiking conditions with milder temperatures and vibrant foliage.

faqs.question: Is the Charco la Pringue circular trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on trails within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, but they must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Always check local regulations before your visit.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the National Park?
faqs.answer: There is typically no entrance fee or specific permit required to access the general hiking trails within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, including the Charco la Pringue route. However, always respect park regulations and guidelines.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route of Charco la Pringue is integrated into the sixth stage of the 'Bosques del Sur' (Southern Forests) long-distance trail (GR-247). Additionally, a section of the route covers approximately 42% of the official trail [CIMA JA07] Collado Agua de los Perros.
